data.title: Oversupplied markets for commodities are setting the stage for lower returns next year - BofA
data.description:
The economic outlook for the U.S. vs. the rest of the world could cast a bearish cloud on raw materials, according to Francisco Blanch, head of Global Commodities Strategy at BofA Securities.

The threat of tariffs is creating a small restocking cycle in the fourth quarter, but international trade tensions may decrease industrial activity in the first half of next year, hurting demand for most cyclical commodities like oil (NYSEARCA:USO), (UCO) and industrial metals (COPX), (CPER), (PICK), said Blanch.

“With non-OPEC supply poised to increase meaningfully in 2025 and demand growth likely to remain steady (or possibly drop materially if Trump tariffs come into play), we see an oil market surplus as incremental supply outpaces the projected global demand increase,” said Blanch. “As prices slip lower, energy exploration private companies could cut back on well completions and ultimately crude oil production growth, eventually allowing for some price support.”

Also, Trump’s “drill, baby, drill” policy “does not necessarily mean a lot more oil output going forward,” said Blanch.

During Trump’s first presidency, U.S. oil production (IEO) growth hit 4M barrels per day but ended up just about 2M barrels per day higher into the end of his term because prices collapsed during Covid-19. Oil production under Biden exceeded the 2M barrels per day.

“The bottom line is that oil supply growth in the U.S. has seen a similar exit rate under every single four-year term during the past 16 years,” said Blanch. “America has never stopped drilling.”

The deficit in aluminum (LMAHDS03:COM) is expected to increase as China's smelters have reached the 45 metric ton capacity cap and operators aside from China “show little appetite to increase production.”

“Meanwhile, copper and zinc (LMZSDS03:COM) mine supply is a constraint to boosting refined production, which should keep the market tight,” said Blanch.

data.title: Microchip Technology sales should bounce back with Sanghi at helm: Citi
data.description:
• Microchip Technology (NASDAQ:MCHP) lowered its quarterly guidance and announced the closure of an Arizona wafer fab yesterday, but the return of former long-term CEO Steve Sanghi signals hope, according to Citi Research.
  • Microchip lowered guidance for the December quarter from a 6%-12% ($1.025B to $1.095B) quarter-over-quarter revenue decline to a 12% decline ($1.025B) due to turns business being lower than expected.
  • "Sales are roughly 20% below pre-COVID levels and given Steve Sanghi's extensive experience in managing MCHP through downturns, we remain confident in MCHP's sales bouncing back faster than peers," said Citi analyst Christopher Delaney.
  • Citi reiterates its Buy rating on the stock and its $82 price target, as it believes Microchip sales will bounce back.

data.title: UBS sees 'isolated signs of market frothiness', says S&P 500 to hit 6,600 by end of 2025
data.description:
There are "isolated signs of market frothiness", but they are far from broad, UBS said on Monday, as it forecast the S&P 500 (SP500) to hit 6600 by December 2025.

The markets have climbed "a wall of worry this year", and that could easily be the story in 2025 as well, Jason Draho, Head of Asset Allocation, UBS Financial Services said, adding that the overarching market narrative this year has swung from soft landing in Q1 to stagflation fears in April to hard landing concerns in early August, and now back to a comfortable soft landing.

The S&P 500 (SP500) has advanced ~26% this year.

"Yet the policy tail risks associated with the incoming (Donald) Trump administration are keeping investors at least slightly on edge, not euphoric. That’s ultimately a good thing for the market outlook because it keeps speculation in check."

The first factor containing market froth is the lack of exuberance about the real economy, Draho said, arguing that while most investors are optimistic about the macro outlook, consumer and business sentiment indicators are well below their peaks in prior cycles.

Additionally, M&A has increased in 2024, but the total value of all deals as a percentage of total market capitalization remains below the historical average.

"Deals are a sign of management confidence in the economy and their own company, and lukewarm M&A activity rhymes with sentiment gauges being well off their peaks," Draho said.

Furthermore, market frothiness often entails investors speculating over future earnings based on paradigm change narratives to justify high valuations, but that condition is not apparent today, he noted. While the AI theme is the obvious candidate, performance over the past six months doesn’t signal exuberance.

Signs of actual froth meanwhile are most evident in crypto prices ((BTC-USD), long positions in S&P 500 futures, and trading volumes in leveraged ETFs, Draho added.

He concluded that one should expect bouts of volatility and corrections similar to 2024 in the coming year, due to concerns about economic fundamentals, not investor euphoria popping.

Elsewhere, Goldman Sachs' Rubner sees S&P 500 charging up to 6,200 in year-end rally.

Here are some exchange-traded funds and mutual funds that can serve as a proxy investment: (NYSEARCA:SPY), (NYSEARCA:VOO), (NYSEARCA:IVV), (SSO), (RSP), (UPRO), (SH), (SDS), (SPXU), (FXAIX), (VFIAX), and (VFFSX).

data.title: Merck gets FDA Breakthrough Therapy designation for sac-TMT
data.description:
Merck (NYSE:MRK) said the FDA has granted Breakthrough Therapy designation for its antibody drug conjugate sacituzumab tirumotecan, also known as sac-TMT, for the treatment of a certain type of lung cancer.

Sac-TMT received the designation for the treatment of patients with advanced or metastatic nonsquamous non-small cell lung cancer with EGFR mutations whose disease progressed on or after TKI and platinum-based chemotherapy, according to a statement.

Merck (MRK) is developing sac-TMT with Kelun-Biotech. The drug is being tested as both a monotherapy and in combination with its drug Keytruda for the treatment of various solid tumors.

Sac-TMT was recently approved in China for the treatment of triple-negative breast cancer. Under the companies’ collaboration agreement, Kelun-Biotech maintains the rights to develop, manufacture and commercialize the product for the Chinese market, including Taiwan.

data.title: Fairfax Financial Holdings Limited CUMULATIVE 5-YEA declares CAD 0.1851 dividend
data.description:
• Fairfax Financial Holdings Limited CUMULATIVE 5-YEA (FFH.PR.G:CA) declares CAD 0.1851/share quarterly dividend.
  • Payable Dec. 31; for shareholders of record Dec. 13; ex-div Dec. 13.
  • See FFH.PR.G:CA Dividend Scorecard, Yield Chart, & Dividend Growth.

data.title: Takeda in licensing agreement with Keros over elritercept
data.description:
• Takeda Pharmaceutical (NYSE:TAK) has brokered a licensing agreement with Keros Therapeutics (NASDAQ:KROS) gaining rights to oncology candidate elritercept outside of China, Hong Kong, and Macau.
  • Elritercept is currently in two phase 2 clinical trials: One in very low-, low-, or intermediate-risk myelodysplastic syndrome; and the other in patients with myelofibrosis. A phase 3 study in transfusion-dependent anemia with very low-, low-, or intermediate-risk MDS is expected to start enrollment shortly.
  • Terms call for Keros to receive a $200M upfront payment as well as milestone payments up to $1.1B plus tiered royalties on net sales. Takeda is responsible for development, manufacturing, and commercialization.
  • Keros said the upfront payment will help sustain operations through Q4 2028.
  • Keros is up 6% in Tuesday morning trading.

data.title: HashiCorp ticks lower as sale to IBM now expected to close in Q1
data.description:
HashiCorp (NASDAQ:HCP) edged lower by 0.3% after the company said it expects its planned sale to IBM (IBM) to close in Q1, according to an 8-K filing on Monday.

The companies originally forecast a Q4 close for the $6.4 billion sale to IBM.

Last week, there was chatter circulating that the deal close may move into Q1 due to UK's antitrust review of the transaction. The UK's Competition and Markets Authority in August informed the companies that it planned to begin a Phase 1 review of the transaction. In July, HashiCorp and IBM said they received a second request from the FTC over its antitrust probe of their merger

IBM in late April agreed to buy HashiCorp for $35 a share in cash. IBM is set to present at the UBS Global Technology Conference on Wednesday.

data.title: Intel stuck between foundry and hard place as it searches for new CEO
data.description:
Intel (NASDAQ:INTC) dismissed CEO Pat Gelsinger, who was a driving force behind improving manufacturing capabilities, including the upcoming 18A node, leaving investors and banking firms divided on the best path forward.

"This is going to be a tough job for anyone to step into (it was a tough situation when Pat showed up, and things look much worse now)," said Bernstein Societe Generale Group analysts, led by Stacy Rasgon, in a note. "The choice for any new CEO would seem to center on what to do with the fabs. But while keeping them feels like deadweight, scrapping them would also be fraught with difficulties around the product roadmap, outsourcing strategy, CHIPS act and political navigation."

One possible replacement has already been revealed. Lip-Bu Tan, a former Intel board of directors member and CEO of Cadence Design Systems (CDNS) from 2009 to 2021, is on the shortlist of potential candidates, according to a report today by Reuters.

Citi Research believes scrapping the foundry dreams remains the best option, at least in terms of the shareholders.

"We believe Intel has a slim chance of succeeding in the merchant foundry business, and the company should exit the merchant foundry business," said Citi analyst Christopher Danely, in a note. "We believe if Intel exits foundry, gross margins could rise to the low to mid 50% range and EPS in the $3.00-$4.00 range, which could translate to a $50-$60 stock."

BofA Securities highlights how the $7.86B CHIPS Act award prevents Intel from fully separating from its foundry business, even if that is in the best interest of the company. Intel must maintain at least 50.1% ownership of Intel Foundry and cannot cede 35% or more of Intel Foundry ownership to a third party if it is listed as a public company.

"INTC continues to cede PC/server shares to Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) and Arm Holdings (ARM)," said BofA analyst Vivek Arya, in a note. "Meanwhile, PC demand outlook remains grim, with Dell (DELL)/HP (HPQ) suggesting a delayed PC refresh cycle into CY25. We also highlight 2H25 costs could get worse for INTC given early start-up costs for its 18A node, which is still to get any external validation from a large fabless customer."

Intel 18A is a new process node from Intel Foundry designed for high-performance computing and mobile applications. It is expected to help Intel compete with semiconductor stalwarts such as TSMC (TSM) and Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF). Intel plans to launch it in 2025.

Intel shares were down 4% by late Tuesday morning trading.

data.title: Pactiv Evergreen jumps on report of possible takeover by Apollo's Novolex
data.description:
Novolex, the packaging maker owned by private-equity firm Apollo Global Management (APO), is weighing a takeover of Pactiv Evergreen (NASDAQ:PTVE), Bloomberg News reported Tuesday, citing people familiar with the matter.

Shares of Pactiv Evergreen (PTVE) jumped as much as 18%.

Novolex is in talks with financial advisers about a potential bid, and no final decision has been made, Bloomberg News reported, citing its unnamed sources.

data.title: Agco rated Equal Weight in new research coverage at Morgan Stanley
data.description:
Agco (NYSE:AGCO) on Tuesday was rated Equal Weight in new research coverage by analysts at financial-services firm Morgan Stanley. They said the maker of farming machinery is fairly valued amid signs of soft demand in the key North American market.

“Heading into the second year of a global ag market downturn, we see a mixed fundamental picture for shares of Agco (AGCO),” Angel Castillo, analyst at Morgan Stanley, said in a December 3 report. “We can't rule out a longer or more pronounced earnings trough at Agco (AGCO) versus peers due to its positioning in North America where it has a relatively higher level of inventory overhang and a tougher road ahead.”

Morgan Stanley set a 12-month price target of $101 a share for Agco (AGCO), based on an estimated multiple of about 17 times next-12-month earnings.

“The market remains generally bearish on the global ag equipment cycle and Agco’s (AGCO) valuation gap to Deere (DE) suggests that it is already pricing in the risk of underperformance,” according to Morgan Stanley. “As such, we see a relatively balanced risk/reward for shares of Agco (AGCO) from here.”

Agco's equipment brands include Massey Ferguson, Challenger, Fendt, Valtra and Grain & Protein.

Agco's (AGCO) stock has declined about 17% since the beginning of the year, contrasting with the 27% gain for the Standard & Poor's 500 stock index (SP500).
